State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · · Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5601              322-6441            322-6444

April 9, 1980


Cheryl J. Kane
Allard, Shelton & O'Connor
100 Pomona Mall West, Sixth Floor
Pomona, CA 91766

Dear Ms. Kane:

     By letter of February 27, 1980, Joanne Pritchard advised
you that the Commission would shortly be discussing the
general subject of the reportability of contributions for
legal expenses about which you had some questions. Although
originally scheduled for discussion on the March 3-4 agenda,
the matter was not taken up until the last meeting on March 31.
This delay accounts for my not having replied to your letter
as soon as our earlier letter might have led you to believe
that I would.

     Your particular question concerns whether the Commission
has drawn a line between contributions that are reportable
and those that are not depending upon whether a committee
"separate and independent of" an election campaign committee
has been created to solicit and to receive them. The Commission has not drawn such a line.

     Instead, it considers the question of the reportability
of contributions for legal expenses on a case-by-case basis
within the statutory context of whether such contributions
are made for political purposes. For example, in S.F.P.C.
Opinions 14 (Douglas Buchanan, No. 78-013, May 1, 1979) the
Commission concluded that contributions for legal expenses
incurred in maintaining a candidate's place on the ballot
had the requisite political purpose to be reportable under
the Act. The Commission reasoned:

     Although payments for the cost of litigation are not
generally thought of as having any connection with
political campaigns, in the circumstances presented
here and in similar circumstances, the litigation costs
are just as key to the success of the campaign as tradi-
tional campaign costs such as mailings and media

Cheryl J. Kane
April 9, 1980
Page Two


advertisements. When expenditures are made to support
litigation aimed at gaining a place on the ballot, or
challenging the results of an election, the expenditures
[or contributions] are made for the purpose of influencing
the outcome of the election in favor of or against a
particular candidate or measure and should be reported.

Opinion at p.3.

(I am enclosing a copy of the Buchanan opinion for your
consideration.) From what I can understand of the factual
context described in your letter, the litigation appears to
be directly related to an election contest and may be, there-
fore, within the scope of the reasoning contained in the
Buchanan opinion.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Thomas M. Sobel
Chief
Legal Division

TMS:plh
Enclosure

ALLARD, SHELTON & O'CONNOR
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
120 POMONA MALL WEST, SIXTH FLOOR
POMONA, CALIFORNIA 91766
(714) 622-1041 AND (213) 964-2393

LEONARD A. SHELTON
MAURICE O'CONNOR
THEODORE P. SHELTON
PAUL A. MAHONEY
JOSEPH P. SOARES
KEITH A. JOHNSON
KEITH S. WALKER
GARY C. WUNDERLIN
CHERYL J. KANE
DAVID BEALES

OF COUNSEL
STEPHEN C. JONES

JOSEPH A. ALLARD
(1887-1968)

NEWPORT BEACH OFFICE
SUITE 200 MONEX INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660
(714) 955-3548

February 21, 1980

Ted Prim
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Opinion/Election Contest Contributions

Dear Mr. Prim:

Earlier today I spoke with Helen Arriola of your Los Angeles office. As a result of that conversation, it was agreed that I should direct my inquiry to your legal division.

Based upon my reading of the Political Reform Act and the administrative regulations thereto, it is my opinion that contributions made and expended solely for the purpose of paying legal fees incurred in an election contest are not required to be reported because the contributions are not made for the purpose of "influencing the action of voters". According to Ms. Arriola, it is the position of the Commission that election contest contributions must be reported if they are made to the election campaign committee, but that no such reporting is required if a separate and independant committee is formed.

In the type of matter to which I make specific reference, election contest contributions that have been made to the election campaign committee have been duly reported by that committee. I want to confirm with your legal division that if election contest committees as established, contributions received by that committee are not subject to the Political Reform Act, but that in the event a separate committee is not established, election contest contributions received by the election campaign committee must continue to be reported by that committee.

When I spoke with Ms. Arriola, we did not discuss whether the same individual could serve as the treasurer of the election campaign committee and the election contest committee without jeopardizing the

Ted Prim
Page Two
February 19, 1980

exempt status of contributions to the election contest committee. I
would appreciate the opinion of your legal division on this question.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Cheryl J. Kane
of
ALLARD, SHELTON & O'CONNOR

CJK/bn

cc: Helen Arriola

February 27, 1980


Cheryl J. Kane
Allard, Shelton & O'Connor
Sixth Floor
100 Pomona Mall West
Pomona, CA 90766

Dear Ms. Kane:

     Thank you for your letter dated February 21, 1980, regarding election contest contributions.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission will be considering the issue of reporting donations for legal expenses as contributions or gifts at its March 3-4 meeting.  Attached is a copy of a memorandum dated February 22, 1980, which will form the basis for the Commission's discussion.

     Tom Sobel, Chief of the Commission's Legal Division, will respond to the questions presented in your letter after the Commission has completed its consideration of this issue.

     If you have any questions or comments, you may contact Mr. Sobel at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Administrative Assistant

Kane Allard, Shelton & O'Connor 100 Pomona Mall West, Sixth Floor Pomona, CA 91766 Dear Ms. Kane: By letter of February 27, 1980, Jeanne Pritchard advised you that the Commission would shortly be discussing the general subject of the reportability of contributions for legal expenses about which you had some questions. Although originally scheduled for discussion on the March 3-4 agenda, the matter was not taken up until the last meeting on March 31. This delay accounts for my not having replied to your letter as soon as our earlier letter might have led you to believe that I would. Your particular question concerns whether the Commission has drawn a line between contributions that are reportable and those that are not depending upon whether a committee "separate and independent of" an election campaign committee has been created to solicit and to receive them. The Commission has not drawn such a line. Instead, it considers the question of the reportability of contributions for legal expenses on a case-by-case basis within the statutory context of whether such contributions are made for political purposes. For example, in 5 FPPC Opinions 14 (Douglas Buchanan, No. 78-013, May 1, 1979) the Commission concluded that contributions for legal expenses incurred in maintaining a candidate's place on the ballot had the requisite political purpose to be reportable under the Act. The Commission reasoned: Although payments for the cost of litigation are not generally thought of as having any connection with political campaigns, in the circumstances presented here and in similar circumstances, the litigation costs are just as key to the success of the campaign as traditional campaign costs such as mailings and media Cheryl J. Kane April 9, 1980 Page Two Advertisements. When expenditures are made to support litigation aimed at gaining a place on the ballot, or challenging the results of an election, the expenditures [or contributions] are made for the purpose of influencing the outcome of the election in favor of or against a particular candidate or measure and should be reported. Opinion at p.3. (I am enclosing a copy of the Buchanan opinion for your consideration.) From what I can understand of the factual context described in your letter, the litigation appears to be directly related to an election contest and may be, therefore, within the scope of the reasoning contained in the Buchanan opinion.
